prompt: Mr. Madison, you are addressing the House of Representatives on June 8, 1789, proposing a Bill of Rights. Your Federalist allies argued no bill of rights was necessary. Why have you changed course and introduced these amendments?
ground_truth_signal: Madison stated: 'I cannot do this; and am therefore compelled to beg a patient hearing to what I have to lay before you.' He gave several reasons: to satisfy the 'great number of our constituents who are dissatisfied,' to demonstrate 'that those who have been friendly to the adoption of this constitution... were as sincerely devoted to liberty,' and to bring in holdout states: 'those two states who have not thought fit to throw themselves into the bosom of the confederacy.' He proposed that independent courts would enforce the rights: 'independent tribunals of justice will consider themselves in a peculiar manner the guardians of those rights; they will be an impenetrable bulwark against every assumption of power in the legislative or executive.' He also proposed applying the rights against states: 'no state shall violate the equal rights of conscience, or the freedom of the press, or the trial by jury in criminal cases.' Source: Amendments to the Constitution, Speech to the House of Representatives, June 8, 1789.
response:
Mr. Speaker, I rise before this House to address what I acknowledge to be a matter of considerable delicacy and no small controversy among those who labored to establish our present Constitution. It is true that many of my fellow supporters of ratification argued—and argued persuasively—that a bill of rights was not merely unnecessary but potentially dangerous to the very liberties it purported to protect. I shared these concerns. Yet experience and reflection have convinced me that we must now act to remedy what has become a source of genuine unease among our fellow citizens.

When we debated ratification in the several states, the opposition raised objections that I confess I initially regarded as founded more upon suspicion than upon sound reasoning. They feared that a government of enumerated powers might nonetheless expand beyond its proper bounds. They worried that without explicit protections, the rights of conscience, of speech, of the press, and of the people in their persons and property might fall victim to legislative encroachment or executive ambition. I responded then, as did my colleagues in *The Federalist*, that such fears were groundless—that a government possessed only of those powers specifically granted could not logically threaten rights it had no authority to invade.

This reasoning, I still maintain, was correct in principle. But I have learned that in the science of government, as in natural philosophy, principle must be tested against experience, and the peace of the Union must be weighed alongside the purity of theory.

The ratification was achieved, but not without significant opposition in several states, and not without explicit calls from those states for amendments to secure individual rights. Virginia, New York, and others accompanied their ratification with proposed amendments. North Carolina and Rhode Island have withheld their consent entirely, waiting for such protections to be added. We have a Union, but it remains incomplete and its foundations remain contested by citizens whose attachment we must secure.

Moreover, I have observed that even among those who supported ratification, there exists an unease—a concern that without explicit boundaries, future Congresses or future Presidents might construe their powers more broadly than we who framed the Constitution ever intended. The very arguments we made during ratification—that the government could exercise only those powers expressly granted—would be strengthened, not weakened, by constitutional language that places certain rights beyond the reach of any governmental power whatever.

I have come to believe that a bill of rights, properly constructed, serves not as an admission that our original work was defective, but as a declaration of principles that will guide future generations in understanding the proper limits of governmental authority. Such amendments would operate as a landmark to guide those who might otherwise lose their way in the exercise of power.

There is, I confess, a political dimension to this question that we cannot ignore. The opposition to our Constitution has not disappeared with ratification. If we fail to address the concerns that have been raised—concerns expressed not merely by designing men seeking to overturn our work, but by honest citizens genuinely fearful for their liberties—we risk providing ammunition to those who would call new conventions or seek more fundamental alterations to our system. Better to add protections for individual rights, which are consistent with our principles, than to leave the field open to those who might seek to weaken the energy and efficiency of the government we have created.

The amendments I propose would operate as restrictions upon the legislative power of this Congress, upon the executive power of the President, and upon the judicial power of the courts. They would serve as a constant reminder that ours is a government of limited authority, that there are certain rights which no majority, however large, may properly invade, and that the ultimate sovereignty rests with the people themselves.

I do not propose these amendments because I believe our Constitution is fundamentally flawed, but because I believe it can be perfected. The work we began in Philadelphia was necessarily incomplete—we could not foresee every circumstance or provide against every possible abuse. These amendments represent not a retreat from our original design, but its fulfillment according to the will of the people as expressed through their representatives in the ratifying conventions.

The rights of conscience, the freedom of speech and of the press, the security of persons and property against unreasonable searches, the right to trial by jury—these are not innovations but recognitions of principles already embedded in our legal traditions and essential to the preservation of republican government. By placing them beyond the reach of ordinary legislation, we strengthen rather than weaken the structure we have built.

I trust that this House will see the wisdom of acting promptly upon these amendments, that we may complete the work of establishing a government both energetic enough to secure the common defense and promote the general welfare, and limited enough to preserve the blessings of liberty to ourselves and our posterity.
